Hope you all had a great week. I got amazing feedback on last week's training. This week, these are my favorite topics. What we're going to be going through is... You can refer to it as... prospecting or qualifying. But it's the most misunderstood of all aspects of sales and influence. And not just in a traditional sales setting. In any setting. When you're just simply trying to interact with another human being. It could be a social setting. It doesn't matter what it is. Even talking to your children. It's not just about the straight line. It's about the goal oriented communication. Meaning it's communication where you have a specific end in mind. You're trying to accomplish something. That's what the straight line is. It's not just for selling in traditional sense. It's for anything where you try to get a certain point. With the straight lines, we are able to divide that up into steps. In the discernible steps that are repeatable. That are easy to learn. And at the end of the day, they're going to get you your outcome. They're going to allow you to get your point across in a way that moves the other person both logically and emotionally. And that causes them to ultimately say yes to buy. So this particular step is the most misunderstood one of all. For a number of different reasons. And it's also a step that's typically shortened, abbreviated, blunted by most salespeople because they get nervous about it. And they feel that if they continue on with this step that they're going to actually break rapport. And in fact, they're actually building rapport. So it's so misunderstood. And now let's move on to the actual training. Here we go. The word that basically best describes this whole process is something called prospecting. That's the term that we use in traditional sales quality. Even though some people call it qualifying and I use this term myself occasionally when I'm talking about prospecting. The big mistake that and the concern I have when people talk about qualifying is they tend to think of it in very narrow terms. Like when you say I'm in the qualifying session and then you need a qualifying end to see if they can afford your product, right. And to make sure that they need your product. They basically that's like that's the main thing. Is it that they financially qualify and do they want what I'm selling and those are the two basic elements that most people focus on when they qualify, right. But when we talk about qualifying, which we really call intelligence gathering with straight line. It's a whole different thing. It's a completely different animal. So whatever possible, I want you to think of this process as intelligence gathering and not just simply qualifying. When you think about it in terms of qualifying, you tend to focus on a couple of things and you miss the boat on the whole thing. Because here's the bottom line. I'm going to tell you this flat out, right. The secret to my success in the front half of a sale meaning before I asked for the order for the first time. Let me draw it out for you. Watch. This is your straight line, right. This is your open with this. The other is that your close, right. And then all boundaries above the whole line. So you also know Pluto, right. You're going to stay in between these two values. The in control of the sale is out of control. So the goal is we want to stay in control. Keep between these values and explain exactly what that means. Because this is where it really starts to get more and more on your prospecting and more on your gathering intelligence. So imagine this is the opening, the first second that you open up your mouth to speak. Whether it's over the phone, maybe you're going door to door, you walk in someone's office, maybe you own a business with your come into you. It's that first moment of contact where you either open up your mouth, the prospect lays eyes on you. It could be even over the internet. Let me just tell you the bottom line here. The internet, I don't know how to explain it. The internet is like, you know, funnels that I used to bringing potential clients, right. That's my online marketing, right. So marketing is what happens essentially to bring in people to your funnel. And then we settle them. We close them to monetize on marketing, to make money. You have to close them. Now, the biggest mistake that internet marketers make, people that sell stuff online is they think that if they perfect internet marketing, they just get so great at it. And their copy is so tight and right. And I teach copywriting as well, right. They think they get it so great. And their funnel is so awesome, they'll never have to speak to a human being. They can just hide behind their computer and reap their awards of being an online market. And it's just fucking bullshit, guys. It's not true at a certain point in time. If you want to make real money you're going to have to speak to another human being. Now, forget about all the business, the non-salesweek, but business aspects. I'm trying to deal with your employees, your credit card processor, your, you know, all the other things that go into building a business. So besides the sales aspect, even in the sales aspect you can't just be online. Because there's a certain cap to how much you can get from solar. Really it's about $2,000 maybe a little bit more. $2,000, $4,000 tops, right. But if you want to really get big money from solar, and I mean that in a positive way I mean, you have something of major value and you want to get a $20,000 sale, $50,000 sale, or bigger than that. You're not going to do without having a conversation with them. So at a certain point in time to take things to the next level you're going to have to either get in the phone see someone in person. It's going to come down to a conversation. And that's when the straight line really kicks in. It covers everything before that and says that the rules that we have the straight line selling are also the straight line market. They're very similar to what you do to attract people as well. But just remember this, to really make money in this world to really build a business, you're going to have to get proficient in communication. Bottom line. So watch, going back to the straight line is that this is that moment where you're just starting. I'm going to have it start. All flight online, in person, over the phone, door to door. You make your first few seconds you have to make that all important first impression. Sharpest attack, enthusiastic sell, expert in your field. Right at your first impression. You're going to make that straight away because without that what happens? Well, you're perceived as being a duller, not an expert in your field, you're not enthusiastic. And people say, you know what? It's not a person worth listening to and they tune out. Now that's one aspect. The other aspect is not only do they say, you know what? It's not really a person worth listening to, but they also say, fuck it, I'm going to be in control because I'm the dominant person on this conversation. I'm the expert. I know more than this schnook. So they naturally they automatically try to take control of the conversation. So what happens if you don't establish yourself as an expert in the beginning, you'll spend a lot of time and control of the sale where the prospect is talking about all sorts of stuff that's not your main to the conversation and I see this happen again and again and again with untrained sales. Okay. So again we take control, right? And then we get to the point here, because it says right about over here, right? Where we start to gather intelligence. We start to ask our prospect questions to see if he financially qualifies for our product, if he truly wants our product, if he needs our product, if he can benefit from our product, what his beliefs are about buying, about sales people in general, what his values are, his hierarchy of values, what is his most important values, and ultimately the most important one of all is where his pain lies. What's that one worry, whether it's financial or any type of worry that keeps him up at night or her up at night and sits in the back of their skull and weighs them down like an anchor basically makes their life just feel heavier and more oppressive. And at the end of the day, the key to really closing especially the tougher prospects is you want to resolve someone's pain in a way. Resolve that pain is by offering our solution which comes next. So we have our first four seconds right and then we transition from that into this prospecting phase. We start gathering intelligence. Okay, we start gathering intelligence. Now when I say dagger intelligence what I'm talking about is you want to find out everything that we might possibly need to know to close the deal. Anything that's germane to the sales process to close the deal. And here's now I'm getting back to the initial thing I said. If there's one secret to the front half of what I do be the front half is what happens up to here when I ask for the order for the first time. So this has been reality on the terrible walks I know right. So let me just redraw this here. The reality here is how we look. If this is the straight line and this is the open and this is the close here. The truth is that the front half of the sales more like a third. It's not over here. It's not usually only a third line. That varies based on your industry and also how many calls are in your sales cycle. The bottom line is you're not typically halfway done when you ask for the order for the first time. And I want you to really be clear on that because emotionally you can't be like I just did this great presentation and I did such a great job. I know he's feeling really good about my product and trust me I'm going to ask for the order and it's a good buy. And then when the prospect says oh it sounds good let me think about it and you're deflated. That's a problem. So you need to emotionally set yourself up for the first objection. The first stall. That's crucial. So then when you get that stall slash objection right. Then we begin the back half of the sale. And the back half of the sale consists of loopholes which I went through if you expect. Okay and I'll go through it again. I'll just let you know hitting things through different items. So remember front half of the sale right starts from the first four seconds and runs to the point where you give your full presentation and you ask for the order. For the first player. Ask for the order. That's front half. Back half or back two thirds starts from when you get hit with the first objection and then you go through the looping process. Now as I say in the back half of the sale the secret source to the back half is looping. Meaning how do you take an objection and use it as an opportunity to build more certainty for each of the three tenths and then seamlessly transition into a close without breaking the report. And that caveat without breaking the report is the most important part. Because that's what most will do in the back half. And they start getting into the objection handling phase. They start getting into some attachment wills or arguing with the client. Because what you're really saying when you overcome an objection is you're wrong. I'm right. Your way of thinking is wrong. Mine is right. And that sets you up to break report. So we don't do that with a straight line. So that's the back half. So what does the front half consist of? The first seconds. It consists of your intelligence gathering phase. Three report building. And then your transition is level four. I need to get my right to the question. Number five is your present presentation and number six would be you absolutely learned. That's the front half. Okay. From here to here. The secret source to the front half is this. Steps two and three. Intelligence gathering and report building. And that's what I really naturally do really, really well. And I want to teach you to do it the same way. It's very easy to do. It's duplicatable. I've taught millions and millions of people how to become expert prospectors. And the term that we use for this is prospecting. And that goes back to the visual of the old scraggly, bearded prospector who's prospecting for gold with a tin pan. He stands over the slide of the stream of this tin pan and he sifts through water. He sifts through more water and more water. Millions of gallons of water flow through his pan. And he's just searching for that one nugget of gold to make it all worth it. Now remember, he's not trying to turn the water into gold. He's sifting out through the water to find gold. And that's what we do. We don't turn non-virus into buyers. We try to sift through all the people that come into our sales funnel and find the ones who are potential buyers and make presentation to those people. And I repeat that again. Take a sip of my rippler. In other words, when we prospect what we're doing, okay, I'm going to say this very slowly, this is the biggest source of time wasting for pretty much not just new sales, but all sales people. When you prospect what you're doing is you're sifting through all the buyers all the potential buyers of gold. All the potential buyers who enter your sales funnel and you're taking, you're extracting out the ones who might potentially buy from you who qualify. That's why be careful with that one. Where they qualify, who might potentially buy and the ones who are not just not qualified, they're not going to buy. They can't afford it. They don't want your product really. They don't need your product. Maybe they've just dragged it to the follow-up by accident. Out of the sales funnel, as quickly and elegantly as possible. Repeat that. You want to eliminate the non-buyers out of your sales funnel as quickly and elegantly as possible. Now, why is that? Very simply, is that the chief mistake that rookie sales people make and even experienced ones to is they get to this point when they're selling and they get a little bit desperate for leads and they start cherishing each individual lead and the operative word there is lead. Not a few tips of leads, not making a buyer. And they value each lead so greatly, they don't want to discard it. So, rather than say, you know what this person is never going to buy from you and getting him out of the funnel, they will actually make a full-blown presentation, finish trust maybe by some slim imagination or some kind of a failure. And that's the source of so much destruction for a sales person because what happens is two things. Number one, not only is it obviously a massive waste of time just to stick to this mathematically. If 100 people enter your sales funnel and it takes you 15 minutes to go through a full-blown presentation to start to finish on average and you decide you're going to, I don't care what the fuck I'm going to make sure that all 100 of these hear my presentation well guess what, you're not going to give 115 minute presentations half of which have no shot of turning into a sale. But you spend all that time, 50 people times 50 minutes, 750 fucking minutes it's 12 hours, okay 12 to 13 hours, okay, 12 to 15 hours, it's a lot of fucking time you spent okay, while you could have taken those people and maybe spent three minutes going through your qualifying eliminated them from the pipeline, save yourself 10 hours. And that over a year my friend it's just the difference between success and failure and here's even worse. So again obviously time-wise it's the biggest fucking waste of time in the world to talk to people who are never going to buy from you. Really, it's just a joke, right? Yeah, people do it all the time, right? The second part and even more destructive believe it or not is the fact that it ends up creating limiting beliefs inside of you. Because you're getting bad intelligence because you know what you say you say, fuck I'm talking to all these people and they just like buying from me I wonder what's wrong with me, maybe I suck at this, am I not a good salesman is my pitch off? Maybe I'm not closing right, maybe I'm doing something wrong here and then you start changing shit or maybe you give up entirely and you know what? You were doing it all based on bad intelligence and you sabotage yourself and you end up thinking you know what maybe I'm just not cut out for sales No, I couldn't have closed them, no one could have closed them. You can't close people who are uncloseable. One thing I always say about the straight line, you hear me say this many times, when you get to the level where you're proficient, not even that's just proficient, you get to this point where you can close anyone who's who's closeable and I promise you that's true, promise you that's true but that doesn't mean you close everybody because there are some people who simply aren't closeable and then there are other people who simply shouldn't be closed for ethical reasons or whatever you just can't help them, right? So that being said if there are some people who aren't closeable, if you're spending your time speaking to them you think, God, I'm just banging my head against the wall, what the fuck? It's the biggest waste of time in the brokerage business we call it getting wooden leads, you know, you give me wood in my pipeline because it looks like a lead it smells like a lead but it ain't a lead, it's not a real lead, okay? So that and this whole process comes down to prospecting intelligence gathering the basics of prospecting, here's what it is, right? You're sifting through all the leads all the people, the human beings that the marketing department handed you in other words, you have marketing on one side of the equation and you have sales on the other, right? Marketing is the process by which you sift through the potential universe of buyers whether it's online marketing, off-TV commercials, telemark, doesn't door to door you're going through all these the gross number of people and then you're netting out the people who say yes, I might be interested they respond to an ad they say, yeah, I'd like to hear about your idea they walk in your door, they click on your so they opt in, they give you the those are the people that come from your marketing efforts, now that could be from a marketing department in a bigger company or you could be as a salesperson doing your own marketing which happens very often, smokeless, where the salesperson ends up being the marketer and the salesperson now when I consult for companies, I do everything possible to have management take back control of marketing because I believe it makes the salesman infinitely more effective if I'm in a company, I would I want to do the marketing for my salesman I want to hand my salesman to qualify these, let them only be closing because I believe the highest value of a salesperson should be selling and they might suck at marketing, I just went through this with a company that I could sell for in the logistics industry and I went through the whole straight line, I was a prod decline on it and we had amazing phenomenal results, phenomenal, right but what they noticed was that at the end of the day, they couldn't get it to go to the highest level because no one could make enough dials, they were running out of leads so I, you know, went back said listen, I told you this was going to happen because once I had them actually going out there and dialing the right way, they could go through a lot of potential people this company had his sales force getting their own leads so they were spending 75% of their day trying to find people to call and then because of that they value each lead so much so I had the manager as a owner, as a test trust me, test it with five or six people and I think give them, buy the leads yourself and hand them a stack of 100 leads per day, a 75 per day okay, they're expected to make 250 calls after they do that with 75, easy because they build up, over a few days you'll have a stack of leads, right? And guess what? His average calls went from 25 calls per day per salesman to 250 10 times so the impact as a business owner of taking back control of your marketing and just giving your sales, look at it this way, you're giving them the fuel to put inside the automobile so the automobile could run without leads sales people become very ineffective, you get it? So again, marketing brings in the leads and then the sales force is there or you or sales person is there to slam them close, to close it, to monetize it right? Listen, you know what Strayer said that because, um, because in the process of developing a straight line product that's just for that, attracting the opposite because there's no better program in reality, it's it's not just about getting late, it's about having someone fall in love with you, seriously! And you know, I get it, when I was young I liked wanting to get late all the fucking time, right Bo? That's what I always say, okay, my son, right? But the boys now I'm in love, you know, and I, and I, and I'm you know, an older guy, you know, it takes a little bit more by aggregate, he's not just kidding, okay? Anyway, Seattle is better, alright? But the point is, is that, um, yeah, what was I saying, okay, the point is, is that it's not just about getting late, it's also about, like, you know, about how about having fun with someone, getting someone to really like you as a human being, you know, inside each of us is an infinitely likeable person, seriously! And, and it's very difficult, I know from my, from my parents, it's difficult to get that point across to someone else, you know, what's my value as a human being, not just money or looks or how good I am in better or not in bed, what about me as a friend, as a person, and, and let me tell you something, there's no better way than to get that point across than through using the straight line system. And by the way, the key's gonna be prospecting, okay, not only is the idea the more women or men you talk to, the more you're going to meet, but also the biggest mistake that people make is what? They know almost the cliche of getting, all the guy fucking dates the 45 measures, talk about himself, you don't give a fuck about, anyone you know what, he didn't ask questions, tell me about yourself, that's what prospecting is about, we talk about intelligence, that's what it is, you get it, it's about asking questions about, and also asking them in a certain way so you're not perceived as the grand inquisitor, like, so tell me how much money you have in the bank right now? How many times do you get laid this week? What do you do for a living? How much money? You miss your balance sheet, whatever it is you're doing, that's not, it's like so tell me, how long have you been out there in the mall before? It's a certain tonality that we use with each question, okay, and based on the tonality you'll get either the right response and you will build rapport or you'll get the wrong response and break rapport. Let me get very specific and I'll bring this back to you, it's just such an important point, okay, so where we are is going to quickly explain the four buying archetypes, let's say it's archetypes is the word, AR, archetypes, right? The four buying archetypes are the people who enter your funnel from your marketing efforts, from your marketing department, and you can afford the four categories, these four categories, you can have what's called your buyers in heat, and these are your best buyers, these are people who need your product, want your product, can afford your product, and also feeling some sort of pain or uncomfortability, and what the fuck is that? It's an earthquake, okay, pain or uncomfortability and they want it now, there's an itch they gotta scratch, they're feeling some uncomfortability, so they want to buy now, they're hot to make their decision, that's your best buyer and it's called buyer in heat, the only problem with that is that not that many of those, because the next category is called buyers in power, and a buyer in power is the same exact thing as a buyer in heat, but the urgency is missing, they need your product, they want your product, they're gonna afford your product, they're gonna buy a product like yours at some point, it's just not hot to buy it right now, it's not urgent, they're not feeling any pain, they're not saying I gotta make this decision right now, my life is out of control in some way, I'm feeling some uncomfortability, so they feel like they're in the power, I'll make the decision when I'm good and ready, right, so obviously this buyer is easy to close with an app order, but these are both great buyers, now in a funnel you might get 15 of these and 35 of those, and I'm 100, those are just the great difficulties on the internet, that's an approximation, that sort of percentage, right, now you have the other two categories, of course, you all look you lose, you look you lose or you're a tire kicker, and you're automatically a tire kicker, I can't help you, right, these are people who disguise themselves as buyers in power, meaning they pretend that they're going to buy with their power, but they have no fucking intention of buying their window shoppers, tire kickers, these are the ultimate time to be buyers in power, they pretend to be buyers in power, but they actually have no intention of buying, they just act like it, they're the most destructive people in your pipeline on these, and these are the ones that I was really talking about, when I talked about that wasting 12 hours, it's on these people who disguise themselves as buyers in power, the main difference between the two is typically financial qualification, meaning these people just simply don't have the money, but they like to fuck in window shop or talk about it, or they're just freaking delusional, okay, that's your third group, and you might have to say, but what are your goals, okay, there's a lot of them there, and then the last group of people who had the mistake I called them, or the people who were dragged into your pipeline, those were people who basically clicked on a site, they didn't really mean to, and they thought it was something else, so they walked into the store and signed in the right place, and wherever they are in a seminar room, they might have been like, friend, just drag them in, come, you got us, but they're just not in the right place, they never had any intention of buying, they don't want to be there, those are the ones that sit unsubscribed when you send them a mail, you get it, those are your mistakes, so there's about maybe 10 of those, okay, more, right, those are your 100 buyers, so what you're doing here, as you take someone down the straight line from the open to the close, you're essentially sifting through these 100 people and extracting at these 50, the buyers in heat and buyers in power, and you're going to continue moving them down the straight line by making full-blown presentations to them, these people are, no, you're going to actually eliminate them from the pipeline as quickly and elegantly as possible, as I said, what are they like, elegantly, you're not saying you're fucking wasting my time, get out of my store, I don't mean that, right, what I'm saying is in a certain place, you know, based on what you said to me, probably it's not going to fit you, and maybe you can go somewhere else, but have a great thanks, and Nicole, on the encounter as quickly as possible, but don't be mean about it or piss someone off, it's just not good for business, alright, but that's the goal, is these people, so let's go back to what we've been talking about, now, do it, check each other's minds, check, check, so watch, here's our straight line, open, close, right, I'll boundary them over below, our first four seconds here, and then we just start asking questions, right, this is our intelligence gathering right here, okay, now when we gather intelligence, there's a second thing that's going on here, and it's the know it's going to be about half of the, and by the way what dictates what percentage of your pipeline is going to be virus and power and virus of heat versus the look you lose in mistakes, is it 50, It does, logical, right? Now, when you gather intelligence, okay, as you're asking these questions, not only are you identifying all things you can know to close the sale, potential sale, right? But you're doing it in a way that gets you into this ultra type of work. How do you do that? How do you step by step? That's really what straight line prospecting is about. I think I said you'll Jim, Jill, you know, basically, this is, this program doesn't work for you. So you have a great day, maybe you might try that store there and that kind of, but what I have here really is not going to work for you. I don't think it's a good fit. They have a lot of you on it. And you end up, and you save the time, the agony, and also the limiting beliefs that come from pitching your heart and soul out and not closing. What am I going to do wrong? Nothing! No one could have closed it. Okay? So, last tip before we go through the 10 things, this is crucial. What you're doing when you gather intelligence is you also identify pain. The rest of the last thing you do is you want to identify where they're pain-loving. What's that worry, that deep worry that sits at the base and that's going to always end out, right? Well, the reason why you're doing that is because that is going to be how you take a buyer in power and turn them into a buyer in heat. A buyer in power is not going to buy from you. You can make a great presentation to them. They'll say, wait, it sounds great. Let me think about it. That's what buyers in power do. They say, I'll make a decision when I'm good and ready. Not going to make it now. That's what buyers in power do. Let me just tell you something. At the end of the day, if you and I went side by side, okay, I promise you I will have closed any salesman in the world in any product, okay? The difference is not going to be the buyers in heat. In other words, you'll close the buyers in heat and sell the line. Those are the easy ones. Anyone, a monkey will close the line. They have a problem. They're feeling pain. They're going to buy from anybody who just has a mix of time to go for the pinch. The difference is I am going to also close pretty much every single buyer in power. While an untrained salesman will not. And that's why the closing rate from a professional straight line pro is so much higher because a straight line pro knows how to take a buyer in power and turn them into a buyer in heat. That's why I take someone who was star-roofed as a buyer in power. They need in my product. They want a product. They can use that product. They have no pain, though. They're not worried about something. Well, maybe they have a worry, but they bury it during denial of that pain. And during this intelligence gathering phase, I uncover and figure out, I find out where that pain is and I'll amplify that pain a little bit. I won't solve the pain yet. I'll say, oh, I can solve. I don't do that. I keep them in that amplify state and that I make my presentation while they're feeling that pain. And then at the very end, I reintroduce that pain and amplify it even more. And that's what looks a buyer in power to buyer heat and bam. That's how I close the tougher buyers also with lowering action threshold and my loop being so, but the key is, is pain. Uncomfortability. What separates these two buyers, heat and power, is buyers and how would not feel pain, whether they're not on their pain. Buyers and heat are what broke through the surface and they got to act down. So the reason why it's so destructive to just say, yeah, I'm qualifying to find out if they need my product and they can afford product, you never found out where their pain was. What's the deepness for? What's the values? What are the beliefs you can use that then to sell? So that's why an average salesman has X closing rate and a great salesman closes much higher because they get the buyers in power as well. Bottom line. Okay. So that's the sort of, you know, the sort of the technical informational side of what we do as prospectors, right? In the straight line. Let's go through our 10 steps here. Number one, first step. I already mentioned that we're going to reinforce it. You are a sister, not an alchemist. In other words, an alchemist is, you know, one of those people from the olden days back in the 15, 60, they used to try to turn lead into gold, turn a base metal, turn something into something else. That's not what we do. We don't turn non buyers into buyers. It's a fool's errand and it's what I've been talking about the whole time. We are sifters. We sift through 100 people to find out the 50 potentially great ones and close the ones that we can. That's what you do. So you're a sifter, not alchemist. You don't turn one thing into another. Number two, always use a strength. In other words, I care whether you're selling in person or over the phone. You always have your own mind. You don't have to be reading from it, but you want to this is going to be the best thing to say in every situation, in every aspect of the sale. There's only going to be the best way to say it. Now, before I go through this, I want to dig into this and say, let me give you the next one. Number three, which is you want to ask permission to ask questions. You don't just start interrogating someone. You don't just say, oh, let me say, so how much do you have in the market? You don't do that. The first thing is, let me just ask you a couple quick questions so I can best serve you. A couple of questions so I don't waste your time. Let me ask you a couple of questions so I can say exactly what you need. Ask for permission and that word so I want to ask you a couple of questions so I don't waste your time. Always use that justifier. So I don't waste time so I can best serve you so I can see exactly what you need that so dramatically improves your outcome. So you want to always use your justifier. Step, now let me go back to the script. When I talk about how the script means, it's going to be a best way to ask for permission and most importantly it's going to be a best set of questions to ask. You're going to have certain questions that you need to ask based on your industry. Now there are some generic questions that apply to every industry but for the most part there's going to be a specialized set of questions that you have to ask someone in order to essentially find out all things you need to know. There's two ways to do that. One, you can fucking wing it. Don't write everything down. Don't plan it out. Just try to fucking say remember them all in the right order. That's ridiculous. Strategic preparation meaning you want to prepare yourself for each sales encounter. Write down exactly what's the best way in your industry to ask for permission to ask questions. Write down all the best questions in exactly the right order. What do I mean by that? The right order? Okay, that goes to the next watch. You want to go always from big picture questions to small picture questions. You don't start off with the minutiae. You go from big picture questions to small picture questions and even more later than that you go from less invasive, this is one of the keys that came in, less invasive to more invasive. This is one I want to slow down. In other words, one of the biggest mistakes that people make when they qualify is they start off asking invasive questions. Tell me how much money do you have in the bank right now? How much do you earn each year? I'll say, what the fuck you? I just met you. If you start off with an invasive question that cuts into what someone's financial qualification is or if they're sick or whatever your industry is whatever it doesn't matter whatever those questions are they're invasive, you want to save those for later on. Start off with your non-invasive questions and questions that any reasonable person would ask. So tell me how long you've been living in the area for? Not what's your home address? How much is your house worth? No, oh so as you know it's a beautiful home. Tell me how long you're living here for? Where'd you move from? So what happens is by going from less invasive questions to more invasive questions when you ask that invasive question tell me how much money do you have in the bank right now? Just a ballpark? They'll answer you! If it's the third or fourth question and you've sort of peeled back the layers of the onions so to speak, right? People will answer very invasive questions in person and over the phone but you have to start off with non-invasive questions first. If you start off trying to pride too deep people do break rapport and the sale ends before it starts. Okay? That's next. Number one. This is now for the sixth. This is a huge one. Right? This is also the key now to the second aspect of prospecting which is building rapport. You need to be using the correct tonality. Each question has its own best tonality and this is the sequence, this is the keys to the kingdom. In other words, if you ask the question one way you'll break rapport and get a negative response. Say this, so tell me what's your biggest worry right now? Say you're sure to zilig. What's going to happen right now if you die right now? How's your family going to suffer from that? Like, fuck you! You can say, so tell me, what's the biggest worst that could happen right now? Have God to get you passed away? How would your family be impacted? See if I care. I feel your pain. I'm just like you. Tell me, empathy, sympathy, that tonality is crucial. Let me just tell you, each individual question is going to have its own best tonality. That's why also you need to script out all your questions first and then say, okay, now what's my best tonality for this one? What's my best tonality for that one? Go through them and say, what is my best tonality for each question and practice it and make sure you ask tonality, the question with the right tonality. Watch what happens. If you ask the question with the wrong tonality here, so what's your deepest worry right now? Let's really, let's really bomb you right now and say, fuck you, you break for poor and it's over. Or say, so tell me, what's your deepest worry right now? What's really keeping you up at night? The guy says, wow, this sales person really cares. They can't help. It's an unconscious response. Remember, tonality cuts through the unconscious and goes straight to the gut, to the unconscious and it creates a gut reaction. This person cares about me. It's absolutely crucial if you ask it, the wrong tonality is screwed. Right tonality is golden, okay? Next, okay? Here's the big one, there's all the big one. And there's, this too, these two are the keys to the Kingdom 4, the building report. Use the correct body language as they respond. In other words, and also especially as they respond. Let me explain. There's something I teach in the straight line system in various parts of it. It's called active listening. Active listening is the art of listening to someone else speak and giving them positive feedback, giving them energy back. So they say, wow, this person understands and they get me to shop. So I ask you a question, right? And you respond, I'm like, uh-huh, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, yep, got it, uh-huh. Uh-huh, mm-hmm. I give you the uh-huhms, the yups, got yup, mm-hmm. I give you that assurance, I get you, I understand you were on the same page and let me just tell you something. There is nothing more powerful for building rapport than to ask someone a question using the right tonality and then say, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, oh, yeah, huh, got it, yep, makes a total set, yep, mm-hmm, got it, yep. Now, there's an actual video of me out there doing a sale. It's a really funny story and I won't tell you, I'll tell you this story next time. It's a long story but there's just, you can actually see this out there and I'll put the links to this thing at the end. It's a sale, or it's me doing a sale, a financial sale to someone in the dairy industry. It's a very long story. The bottom line is, you know, people want to, I close the guy, okay, and here's, I'll tell you the truth. It was an actor. I was being paid to always do research on how I close it, such a high level psychology company, right? And they paid someone and they told this guy, no matter what he says to you, no matter what he does, and how good he sounds, don't buy. Do not say yes. You cannot say yes. And 20 minutes later, the guy says, all right, I'll do it, and he said, and everyone in the room started cracking up and they said, what did you do? How come you didn't say no? It was just too compelling. So I closed the one that wasn't even closed-able using the straight line system. Remember you posted your questions on the Patreon link and this first question is a two-part question. And he says, you say some people spend an hour closing someone when it can be done in five minutes. What do they do wrong? And is it true that there's virtually no difference in closing a $2,000 account than a $30,000 account? Well, the same difference. Okay, so you're using numbers of $2,000, $30,000 without qualifying what, you know, $2,000 to one person is an awful lot of money and $30,000 to another person is nothing. Like my friend James, you know, he spends $30,000, he sneezes and $30,000 comes out of his bucket and he's on the floor and he doesn't can walk away. He's a billionaire, right? Other people, $2,000 is their life fucking savings. So you can't say like let's just try to reframe the question here. You know, is there a difference between selling big ticket items and small ticket items? The answer is yes and no. The same core elements must line up. Whether it's a $500 product or a $5,000 product, they must you know, the three tens, right? They have to love the product logically, emotionally, trust to connect with you, trust to connect with the company that stands behind the product. Then you have to lower their action threshold if necessary and add on paying it there and denial of the pay. Those, so it's always the same that those things have to happen. With the bigger ticket sales, sometimes there's more things involved as well. Like for instance, there's multiple decision makers involved. And also it's not just money. It's not just the money that dictates how carefully someone needs to make a decision. It's what is the impact when the person has to implement that decision. Someone buying something for themselves when it's going to be a luxury item is going to be very different than someone investing $30,000 a new piece of infrastructure that everyone's going to have to adopt in their company and everyone's going to have to use and people will sign off on it. If it doesn't work, it's a fucking disaster. So it's not like that. You know, you're focusing on money as being the deal-end of what makes one sale one million. It's not. It has to do with you could be selling a $500 product, but that product is going to have even more implications than a $50,000 purchase that someone can make. And it's that the implications of what has to happen in terms of what does it impact in the company if it goes wrong that creates the tougher sales. So it's not so much money, but there are differences in the way how much marketing collidery, how much more information has to be sent to them, how many steps are in your sales process. Okay, so those are the big difference, but it's not just on money. That was the first part. What was the second part? That was the second part. Is there a limit to how many questions you should ask? The limit is you don't want to ask one more question than you have to and don't ask one less question than you have to. Work backwards, the street light always works backwards. What things do you legitimately need to know? For instance, when I I have a mastermind program for very high end people and Bannler if you'd like to join send you can see, and by the way if you want to join it, you have to qualify for it for very successful people, right? And it's, you know, 11 people see it but when I send out an email about that to my high end players and I say fill out this questionnaire, I write out all the questions and I go through the questions and say do I really need to ask this? Or am I just asking it for my own fucking health? Am I asking because I just want to ask a question? Does it really give me legitimate intelligence I need to close? Don't ask questions just for the sake of asking questions. Ask what you need to ask and not one question more but make sure that you ask an exhaustive line of questioning. There's going to be things you need to know. Find out what they are and work backwards. Okay? One more? Yeah, do one more. I want to pick a kid one. Drum roll on. One more, one more, one more.
